perturbation.original_text: (g) Company Policies and Indemnification. (i) The Parties acknowledge that each of the MG Capital Designees and each other member of the 2020 Director Slate will be governed by the same protections and obligations as other non-employee directors of the Company, including, without limitation, confidentiality, conflicts of interest, related party transactions, fiduciary duties, codes of conduct, trading and disclosure policies, and other governance guidelines and policies of the Company (collectively, "Company Policies"), and shall have the same rights and benefits as other non-employee directors of the Company, including without limitation with respect to insurance, indemnification, compensation and fees. (ii) The Parties acknowledge that to the extent they have not already done so, within three (3) business days of the date hereof, the Company shall enter into an indemnification agreement with each of the New Directors in the form attached as Exhibit 10.20 to the Company's Annual Report on Form 10-K, previously filed with the SEC on March 16, 2020.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) Company Policies and Indemnification. (i) The Parties acknowledge that each of the MG Capital Designees and each other member of the 2020 Director Slate will be governed by confidentiality, conflicts of interest and trading policies of the Company (collectively, "Company Policies"), and shall have the same rights and benefits as other non-employee directors of the Company with respect to compensation and fees. (ii) The Parties acknowledge that to the extent they have not already done so, the Company may enter into an indemnification agreement with each of the New Directors.
perturbation.explanation: Removing explicit references to fiduciary duties and obligations and weakening the commitment to indemnification introduce a legal contradiction. Delaware law imposes specific fiduciary duties on directors, and omitting these obligations and related party transaction guidelines undermines those legal protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law explicitly outlines the fiduciary duties of corporate directors, including the duty of care and the duty of loyalty. By removing the explicit mention of these duties, the contract suggests that directors are not bound by these obligations, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits explicit references to fiduciary duties and related party transaction guidelines, which are essential obligations for directors under Delaware law, as indicated by the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly list 'fiduciary duties', it states that 'the business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors', implying a broad scope of responsibility that includes fiduciary duties. The omission of these duties in the modified contract weakens the directors' obligations and creates a legal contradiction by potentially allowing actions that would violate these implied duties, especially regarding conflicts of interest and related party transactions. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this removal undermines legal protections.

perturbation.original_text: (xi) institute, solicit, assist or join, as a party, any litigation, arbitration or other proceeding against or involving the Company or any of its or their current or former directors or officers (including derivative actions) in order to effect or take any of the actions expressly prohibited by this Section 3; provided, however, that for the avoidance of doubt the foregoing shall not prevent the MG Capital Parties, and their Affiliates or Associates from (A) bringing litigation to enforce the provisions of this Agreement, (B) making counterclaims with respect to any proceeding initiated by, or on behalf of, the Company against the MG Capital Parties, their Affiliates or their Associates, (C) bringing bona fide commercial disputes that do not relate to the subject matter of this Agreement, (D) complying with a validly issued legal process or (E) exercising statutory appraisal, dissenters or similar rights under applicable law;
perturbation.changed_text: (xi) institute, solicit, assist or join, as a party, any litigation, arbitration or other proceeding against or involving the Company or any of its or their current or former directors or officers (including derivative actions) in order to effect or take any of the actions expressly prohibited by this Section 3; provided, however, that for the avoidance of doubt the foregoing shall not prevent the MG Capital Parties, and their Affiliates or Associates from (A) bringing litigation to enforce the provisions of this Agreement, (B) making counterclaims with respect to any proceeding initiated by, or on behalf of, the Company against the MG Capital Parties, their Affiliates or their Associates, (C) complying with a validly issued legal process ;
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit exception for 'bringing bona fide commercial disputes that do not relate to the subject matter of this Agreement' and 'exercising statutory appraisal, dissenters or similar rights under applicable law' creates a legal contradiction. Delaware law grants shareholders the right to bring certain types of legal actions against a corporation, including actions related to appraisal rights. Removing these rights restricts the MG Capital Parties legal abilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 262 of the Delaware General Corporation Law outlines the appraisal rights of stockholders in certain mergers and consolidations. By omitting the allowance for exercising statutory appraisal rights, the agreement attempts to limit rights granted by law.

General Corporation Law Subchapter IX. Merger, Consolidation or Conversion Â§ 251. Merger or consolidation of domestic corporations [For application of this section, see 79 Del. Laws, c. 327, Â§ 8; 80 Del. Laws, c. 265, Â§ 17; and 82 Del. Laws, c. 256, Â§ 24]. (a) Any 2 or more corporations of this State may merge into a single surviving corporation, which may be any 1 of the constituent corporations or may consolidate into a new resulting corporation formed by the consolidation, pursuant to an agreement of merger or consolidation, as the case may be, complying and approved in accordance with this section. (b) The board of directors of each corporation which desires to merge or consolidate shall adopt a resolution approving an agreement of merger or consolidation and declaring its advisability. The agreement shall state: (1) The terms and conditions of the merger or consolidation; (2) The mode of carrying the same into effect; (3) In the case of a merger, such amendments or changes in the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation as are desired to be effected by the merger (which amendments or changes may amend and restate the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation in its entirety), or, if no such amendments or changes are desired, a statement that the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation shall be its certificate of incorporation; (4) In the case of a consolidation, that the certificate of incorporation of the resulting corporation shall be as is set forth in an attachment to the agreement; (5) The manner, if any, of converting the shares of each of the constituent corporations into shares or other securities of the corporation surviving or resulting from the merger or consolidation, or of cancelling some or all of such shares, and, if any shares of any of the constituent corporations are not to remain outstanding, to be converted solely
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allowed the MG Capital Parties to exercise statutory appraisal, dissenters, or similar rights under applicable law. The changed text removes this exception, which could be interpreted as restricting rights granted by law. The Law Explanation states that Section 262 of the Delaware General Corporation Law outlines the appraisal rights of stockholders. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Delaware Code Title 8 which includes section 262, supporting the explanation that the changed text attempts to limit rights granted by law.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT WILL BE GOVERNED IN ALL RESPECTS, INCLUDING VALIDITY, INTERPRETATION AND EFFECT, BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO THE CHOICE OF LAW PRINCIPLES OF SUCH STATE.
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT WILL BE GOVERNED IN ALL RESPECTS, INCLUDING VALIDITY, INTERPRETATION AND EFFECT, BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO THE CHOICE OF LAW PRINCIPLES OF SUCH STATE.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Delaware to New York introduces a legal contradiction relating to corporate governance matters. As the company is incorporated in Delaware, Delaware law primarily governs internal corporate affairs including fiduciary duties. While New York law can govern contracts, specifying it for the entire agreement, including validity, interpretation, and effect, potentially conflicts with the established principles of internal affairs doctrine. This doctrine generally holds that the law of the state of incorporation governs internal corporate governance matters.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct citation, the Internal Affairs Doctrine is a well-established principle in corporate law, derived from various Delaware court decisions and statutes (e.g., 8 Del. C. § 109(b) regarding bylaws). Changing the governing law to New York attempts to supersede Delaware law on internal corporate matters, which is a contradiction of this doctrine. By changing the governing law from Delaware to New York, matters of internal corporate governance, which are legally defined by Delaware corporate law, can now be superseded by New York State Law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from Delaware to New York, which contradicts the principle that the law of the state of incorporation (Delaware in this case) governs internal corporate affairs. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this contradiction with the Internal Affairs Doctrine. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 8 Del. C. 1953, Â§ 101, establishes the right to incorporate under Delaware law, indicating that Delaware law has primary jurisdiction over corporations formed within the state regarding internal governance. By changing the governing law to New York, the modified contract attempts to supersede this established legal precedent.
